papers.title: 面向血液动力学的光声显微 成像技术 及其 应用研究
papers.abstract_original:
III 
 
Abstract 
Neuroimaging techniques are effective tools for investigating brain 
function. In the current neuroimaging field, mature technologies exist such as 
two-photon fluorescence imaging, wide -field fluorescence imaging, and 
electroencephalography, which can directly image and characterize micro -, 
meso -, and macro -level neural act ivity. However, for the other vital system in 
the brain, the vascular system, the current mature functional magnetic 
resonance imaging and functional ultrasound technologies only support research 
on main vessels or average hemodynamics. This creates ongoin g controversies 
regarding various blood transport issues related to brain function and makes it 
challenging to cross -validate neural activity and hemodynamic information at 
the microvascular level. Therefore, to further uncover the mystery of this 
complex system, there is an urgent need in neuroscience for a new imaging 
technique that can address hemodynamics and support micro and meso -level 
imaging. 
Photoacoustic microscopy is an effective solution to this problem. This 
imaging technique relies mainly on t he photoacoustic effect, imaging by 
receiving ultrasound signals emitted by chromophores excited by pulsed light. 
It has inherent characteristics suitable for biological imaging, such as label -free, 
radiation -free, and non -invasive features. The imaging co ntrast comes from the 
chromophore's absorption ability of laser, and the amplitude of the resulting 
photoacoustic signal directly indicates the concentration of hemoglobin , which 
is closely related to brain activity. In addition, the imaging resolution of this 
technology depends on the numerical aperture of the focusing objective and can 
reach submicron or even nanometer levels. The imaging speed can cover 
hemodynamic fluctuation frequencies, and the imaging field of view can also 
cover the mouse cortex. Ho wever, even though all the performance indicators of 
photoacoustic microscopy can meet the needs of brain function research, the 
imaging system is typically complex, voluminous, and inflexible, which limits 
its development in the field of neuroscience. 
http://kc.sustech.edu.cn

Abstract 
IV 
 
Therefore, this article conducted research on photoacoustic microscopy 
brain imaging technology, including feasibility demonstration, imaging system 
development, and analysis method establishment. Feasibility demonstration is 
the theoretical basis for all sub sequent research, which mainly deduces the 
generation principle of photoacoustic signals and the linear relationship 
between photoacoustic signals and chromophore concentration, demonstrates 
the feasibility of using photoacoustic signals to reflect hemoglo bin 
concentration, and the signal homogeneity of photoacoustic imaging technology 
and existing mature neuroimaging techniques. Based on theoretical research, 
this article developed a rotating optical resolution photoacoustic microscope 
suitable for imaging hemoglobin in the mouse cortex, with a two -dimensional 
scanning mirror and a line -focused ultrasound detector as the core devices and a 
novel rotating scanning mechanism as the imaging basis. Compared with 
classical photoacoustic microscopy imaging system s, the system integration and 
usability have been significantly improved, and high spatiotemporal resolution 
imaging of micro vessels within the mouse cortex can be easily achieved. In 
addition, this article added a dual -wavelength input module to the imag ing 
system to add blood oxygen saturation imaging function, and used 
photoacoustic correlation spectroscopy to add blood flow velocity imaging 
function. The cortical vascular photoacoustic images obtained by the imaging 
system are processed using both stru ctural and functional analysis methods, 
which can extract various structural indicators such as vascular area ratio, 
vascular length, vascular branching number, total number of vessels, and 
vascular diameter, as well as various brain activity functional in formation such 
as low -frequency amplitude, neurovascular coupling, and cortical functional 
connectivity, for a comprehensive analysis of the cortical vascular network 
from multiple perspectives. 
Based on the high -performance photoacoustic microscopy imagin g system 
and the accompanying data analysis methods, this article conducted in -depth 
research on multiple animal models, such as global epilepsy, focal epilepsy, 
local brain electrical stimulation, brain glioma, and local limb stimulation in 
mice. Specific ally, this article used functional analysis to demonstrate the vast 
difference in abnormal brain activity and functional connectivity between two 
http://kc.sustech.edu.cn

Abstract 
V 
 
epilepsy models and the propagation path of hemodynamic abnormalities in 
focal epilepsy. At the same time, by comparing the brain activity responses of 
focal epilepsy and local brain electrical stimulation, it revealed the long -range 
functional connections between the somatosensory cortex and motor cortex in 
the cerebral cortex. In addition, it quantitatively demo nstrated the pro -growth 
effect of brain gliomas on the cortical vascular network, the interference effect 
on cortical hemodynamic responses, and the destructive effect on regional and 
hemispheric functional connections. 
In summary, this article designed an d developed a photoacoustic 
microscopy imaging system suitable for mouse brain function research, as well 
as accompanying data analysis methods, and conducted brain function research 
on various mouse models. The theoretical analysis and application researc h 
conducted in this article fully demonstrate the brain function research 
capabilities of the photoacoustic microscopy brain imaging technology 
developed in this article, laying a solid foundation for the progress of imaging 
systems in photoacoustic imagin g and providing a new method for mesoscopic 
brain functional imaging based on hemodynamics for neuroscience research.
papers.abstract_summary_zh: 这篇文献主要围绕“脑肿瘤”相关问题展开，核心对象是“面向血液动力学的光声显微 成像技术 及其 应用研究”所对应的方法、材料或系统。作者重点讨论了该方向中的关键挑战、解决思路以及验证结果，适合用来快速把握这项工作的研究重点和应用价值。

papers.title: Contents lists available atScienceDirect Photoacoustics
papers.abstract_original:
The Hessian-based Frangi vesselness filter is commonly used to enhance vasculature in optoacoustic (photo-
acoustic) images, but its accuracy and limitations have never been rigorously assessed. Here we validate the
ability of the filter to enhance vessel-like structures in phantoms, and we introduce an experimental approach
that uses measurements before and after the administration of gold nanorods (AuNRs) to examine filter per-
formanceinvivo. We evaluate the influence ofcontrast, filter scales, angular tomographiccoverage, out-of-plane
signals and light fluence on image quality, and gain insight into the performance of the filter. We observe the
generationofartifactualstructuresthatcanbemisinterpretedasvesselsandprovide recommendationsto ensure
appropriate use of Frangi and other vesselness filters and avoid misinterpretation of post-processed optoacoustic
images.

papers.title: 3064 IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON MEDICAL IMAGING, VOL. 39, NO. 10, OCTOBER 2020
papers.abstract_original:
— By localizing microbubbles (MBs) in the
vasculature, ultrasound localization microscopy (ULM) has
recently been proposed, which greatly improves the spatial
resolution of ultrasound (US) imaging and will be help-
ful for clinical diagnosis. Nevertheless, several challenges
remain in fast ULM imaging. The main problems are that
current localization methods used to implement fast ULM
imaging, e.g., a previously reported localization method
based on sparse recovery (CS-ULM), suffer from long data-
processing time and exhaustive parameter tuning (optimiza-
tion). To address these problems, in this paper, we propose
a ULM method based on deep learning, which is achieved
by using a modiﬁed sub-pixel convolutional neural network
(CNN), termed as mSPCN-ULM. Simulations and
in vivo
experiments are performed to evaluate the performance of
mSPCN-ULM. Simulation results show that even if under
high-density condition (6.4 MBs/mm
2), a high localization
precision (∼28µm in the lateral direction and ∼24µmi nt h e
axial direction) and a high localization reliability (Jaccard
index of 0.66) can be obtained by mSPCN-ULM, com-
pared to CS-ULM. The
in vivo experimental results indicate
that with plane wave scan at a transmit center frequency
of 15.625 MHz, microvessels with diameters of ∼17µm can
be detected and adjacent microvessels with a distance
of ∼42µm can be separated. Furthermore, when using
GPU acceleration, the data-processing time of mSPCN-ULM
can be shortened to ∼6 sec/frame in the simulations and
∼23 sec/frame in the
in vivoexperiments, which is 3-4 orders
of magnitude faster than CS-ULM. Finally, once the network
is trained, mSPCN-ULM does not need parameter tuning to
Manuscript received February 11, 2020; revised March 29, 2020;
accepted March 29, 2020. Date of publication April 9, 2020; date of cur-
rent version September 30, 2020. This work was supported in part by the
National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant 61871263,
Grant 61571281, and Grant 81371604.
(Corresponding authors: Xin Liu;
Jianwen Luo.)
Xin Liu was with the School of Communication and Information
Engineering, Shanghai University , Shanghai 200444, China. He is now
with the Academy for Engineering and T echnology , Fudan University ,
Shanghai 200433, China (e-mail: xinliu.c@gmail.com).
Tianyang Zhou and Mengyang Lu are with the Institute of Biomedical
Engineering, Shanghai University , Shanghai 200444, China, and also
with the School of Communication and Information Engineering, Shang-
hai University , Shanghai 200444, China (e-mail: yozty@shu.edu.cn;
1342256138@qq.com).
Yi Y ang, Qiong He, and Jianwen Luo are with the Department
of Biomedical Engineering, School of Medicine, Tsinghua Univer-
sity , Beijing 100084, China (e-mail: yang-y16@mails.tsinghua.edu.cn;
cmuheqiong@163.com; luo_jianwen@mail.tsinghua.edu.cn).
This article has supplementary downloadable material available at
http://ieeexplore.ieee.org, provided by the authors.
Color versions of one or more of the ﬁgures in this article are available
online at http://ieeexplore.ieee.org.
Digital Object Identiﬁer 10.1109/TMI.2020.2986781
implement ULM. As a result, mSPCN-ULM opens the door
to implement ULM with fast data-processing speed, high
imaging accuracy, short data-acquisition time, and high
ﬂexibility (robustness to parameters) characteristics.
Index Terms — Convolutional neural networks, deep
learning, sub-pixel convolution layer, super-resolution
ultrasound imaging, ultrasound localization microscopy.
I. I NTRODUCTION
U
LTRASOUND (US) imaging is a widely used modality
in clinical diagnosis [1]. However, the spatial resolution
of US imaging is theoretically constrained to approximately
half the wavelength of the used sound wave. In clinical appli-
cations, ultrasound wavelengths are generally between 0.1 mm
and 1 mm, leading to a sub-millimeter spatial resolution [2].
Imaging with a shorter wavelength (or, a higher frequency) can
improve the spatial resolution, however, it results in a reduced
penetration depth.
To overcome the limitation, inspired by optical super-
resolution localization microscopy [3]–[5], e.g., stochastic
optical reconstruction microscopy (STORM) or photoacti-
vated localization microscopy (PALM), researchers have suc-
cessfully applied the localization imaging concept to US,
referred to as ultrasound localization microscopy (ULM)
or super-resolution ultrasound imaging (SR-US) [6]–[16].
By combining ultrasound microbubble (MB) as contrast agents
with the localization methods, ULM breaks the fundamental
acoustic diffraction limit and signiﬁcantly improves the spatial
resolution by more than an order of magnitude, which is
helpful for clinical diagnosis.
When implementing ULM, the use of localization meth-
ods will directly affect the imaging performance. In cur-
rent ULM imaging methods, MBs in the vasculature are
generally resolved by the single-emitter localization meth-
ods [7]–[11], e.g., the Gaussian ﬁtting method and the Centroid
method [7]–[9]. When using these localization methods, it is
necessary to reduce the possibility that the distance between
two MBs is less than the resolvable limit, i.e., MBs in each
frame have to be sufﬁciently sparse so that the individual
centers can be identiﬁed [7]. As a result, a low MB concen-
tration is typically needed, leading to a long data-acquisition
time required to accumulate enough MBs. This limits the
applications of ULM in fast biomedical imaging ﬁeld.
One way to improve the imaging speed is the use of tech-
niques based on time correlation, e.g., super-resolution optical
0278-0062 © 2020 IEEE. Personal use is permitted, but republication/redistribution requires IEEE permission.
See https://www.ieee.org/publications/rights/index.html for more information.
Authorized licensed use limited to: Soochow University. Downloaded on March 02,2026 at 05:57:20 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. 
LIU et al.:D L F O R U L M 3065
ﬂuctuation imaging (SOFI) [17]. By exploiting the temporal
ﬂuctuations in acoustic signals, Bar-Zion et al. implemented
fast vascular ultrasound imaging with enhanced spatial resolu-
tion [18]. However, it is worth noting that in SOFI-based ULM
methods [18], [19], the spatial resolution is usually restricted
to lower order cumulants (typically 2 order) [7], leading
to a limited improvement in spatial resolution compared to
the single-emitter localization methods. As a result, it does
not allow microscopic resolutions [7]. Considering that MBs
are generally sparely distr ibuted in each frame compared to
the imaging region, recently, l ocalization methods based on
compressed sensing (CS) [12], [13] (termed as CS-ULM) have
been proposed and successfully applied to ULM. In our previ-
ous work, by applying CS to US imaging model, we demon-
strated that the overlapped MBs with a high density could be
efﬁciently identiﬁed [12]. As a result, it reduces the number
of imaging frames required for accumulating enough MBs,
leading to an improved data-acquisition speed. However, when
using CS-ULM, there is a fundamental problem that the imag-
ing performance depends on the reconstruction parameters.
Unfortunately, up to now, there is no clear consensus on how
to ﬁnd the “best” parameters, which requires user expertise
and increases the processing complexity. In addition, CS-ULM
is computationally intensive because of the complex iterative
procedure, which leads to a large computational burden and
inﬂexibility for clinical applications.
Recently, deep learning (DL) [20]–[22] has shown great
potential in solving medical imaging problems including x-ray
computed tomography (XCT) [23]–[25], magnetic resonance
imaging (MRI) [26], [27], positron emission computed
tomography (PET) [28], [29], photoacoustic tomography
(PAT) [30]–[32], and super-resolution optical microscopy
(SROM) [33]–[35]. In the US literature, deep neural networks
have been successfully used for noise reduction [36] and
clutter suppression [37]. Furthermore, Feigin et al. employ
convolutional neural network (CNN) to estimate the speed of
sound in human body [38]. In ULM, CNN is also designed
as spatio-temporal ﬁlter to efﬁ ciently extract MBs from tis-
sue [39]. On the other hand, in the ﬁeld of US imaging, deep
neural networks are developed to reconstruct US images from
limited radio-frequency (RF) data [40], [41], to implement
high-quality beamforming [42], [43], and to correct artifacts in
multi-line acquisition [44] and transmission [45]. In addition,
DL has been used to improve the imaging performance of
scanning acoustic microscopy [46], ultrasound reﬂection imag-
ing [47], plane wave compounding imaging [48], and Doppler
imaging [49].
Intuitively, DL may also be used in ULM imaging to
improve the imaging performance and the ﬂexibility of imple-
mentation. In ULM, two kinds of images are available. One
corresponds to the experimentally acquired US images at
low-resolution (LR) while the second depicts the correspond-
ing images containing the true locations of the MBs at super-
resolution (SR). By training with the above data, DL is capable
of learning a nonlinear mapping between the experimental
images and the true locations of the MBs. This mapping
provides the possibility of directly estimating the locations
of the MBs from the acquired US images. In another word,
DL will be “intelligent” that it is capable of utilizing this
mapping knowledge to directly implement ULM, as demon-
strated by Sloun et al. [14], [15]. It should be noted that to
implement ULM, i.e., to achieve a super-resolution US image,
it is necessary to increase the resolution of the LR image
(the experimentally acquired U S images) to match that of the
SR image (ULM). In the work from Sloun et al. [14], [15],
the image resolution is increased by using the standard up-
sampling and transposed convolutions, which are implemented
based on an encoder-decoder network, similar to the U-Net
architecture.
To effectively map the LR image into the SR image,
Shi et al. [50] develop a sub-pixel CNN for image recovery,
in which sub-pixel means dividing each pixel in the LR image
into the corresponding r
2 (r × r) sub-pixels according to an
upscale factor r. According to [50], by using the sub-pixel
convolution layer, on one hand, the computational and memory
complexity can be reduced when performing the upscaling
operations. On the other hand, it is helpful for learning a more
complex LR-to-SR mapping.
Inspired by the work of Shi et al., in this paper, we propose
a new DL method for ULM imaging, which is implemented
based on a modiﬁed sub-pixel CNN architecture, termed as
mSPCN-ULM. For this task, a sub-pixel CNN architecture
is adopted. Especially, according to [51], residual blocks
are also incorporated into the sub-pixel network for further
improving the training efﬁciency. We train the network on
simulated data, where the LR US images are considered as
the input to the network and the true locations of MBs are
considered as the labels. Sequentially, the trained network is
directly applied to both simulated and experimental data. The
results of the simulations and in vivo experiments conﬁrm that
mSPCN-ULM makes the possibility of implementing ULM
imaging with fast data-processing speed, short data-acquisition
time, high imaging accuracy, and high ﬂexibility of imple-
mentation (robustness to parameters). The source code and
data for this work are available on IEEE DataPort (DOI:
10.21227/jdgd-0379).
The rest of the paper is organized as follows. In Section II,
the methods are described, including mSPCN-ULM archi-
tecture, loss function, network training, quantitative analysis,
and other ULM methods. In Section III, the training data,
and experimental designs of the simulations and in vivo
experiments are detailed. In Section IV , the corresponding
experimental results are analyzed. Finally, we discuss the
relevant results and draw a conclusion in Section V.
II. M
ETHODS
As mentioned above, to implement ULM, the experimen-
tally acquired LR US images need to be upscaled to the
SR space. Generally, there are two kinds of methods to
implement the upscaling operation. The ﬁrst kind of methods
is to increase the resolution of the LR images (the input data)
before the network. After that, the scaled image is used as
the input to the network. In the second kind of methods,
the upscaling operation is implemented gradually in the middle
of the network. It is worth noting that the ﬁrst kind of methods
Authorized licensed use limited to: Soochow University. Downloaded on March 02,2026 at 05:57:20 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. 
3066 IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON MEDICAL IMAGING, VOL. 39, NO. 10, OCTOBER 2020
Fig. 1. Schematic diagram depicting the principle and the overall
structure of the used network. The experimentally acquired US images
are used as the input to the network, and the corresponding images
containing the true locations of MBs are used as the output. An efﬁcient
sub-pixel convolutional neural network with modiﬁcations of adding
residual blocks is used to train the above data. The compositions of
the residual blocks and the principle of the pixel shufﬂe operation of
the sub-pixel convolutional layer are shown in the purple and red dotted
boxes, respectively . Here,
r is the upscale factor.
increases the computational complexity since the processing
speed of convolutional networks directly depends on the size
of the input image. In additi on, the interpolation methods
typically used to scale the input images before the network
do not bring additional information to the network, which may
affect the obtained imaging performance. A comparison of the
above upscaling schemes implemented on a U-Net architecture
is demonstrated in Section I.E of the Supplementary Material.
Based on the above considerations, in this work, the image
resolution is increased only at the end of the network. Here,
it is implemented using an efﬁcient sub-pixel convolution
layer similar to [50], which is capable of further reducing the
computational complexity of upscaling operation and learning
a better LR-to-SR mapping. Fig. 1 shows the overall structure
of the used network.
A. Network Architecture
The overall structure of CNN we used follows an efﬁ-
cient sub-pixel architecture [50]. In addition, to improve the
training efﬁciency and reduce the training error, the residual
blocks [51] are added. Brieﬂy, as shown in Fig. 1, the network
consists of 13 convolution layers. The ﬁrst convolution layer
is used to extract shallow layer features of the image. Then,
the 10 convolution layers in the intermediate residual structure
are used for local residual learning. The 12th convolution layer
is connected to the 1st convolu tion layer for global residual
learning. The 13th convolution layer, i.e., the sub-pixel con-
volution layer at the end of the network, is used to implement
up-sampling operation. The initial input to the network is the
experimentally acquired US image x and the corresponding
ﬁnal output is the MB localization image y. Here, the sizes
for x and y are different in the following simulations and
in vivo experiments. In the simulations, the sizes of x are set
mainly based on assessment purpose and computational cost.
In the in vivo experiments, the sizes of the input data are equal
to the sizes of the experimentally acquired US data. Details
on the network are described below.
1) Feature Extraction : Feature extraction of the image is per-
formed by the convolution layer followed by a nonlinear acti-
vation function (ReLU) in the LR space. In detail, the feature
extraction layers of the entire network use 3 × 3 convolution
kernels, except the ﬁrst layer that uses 9×9 convolution kernel.
The functions of the feature extraction layers can be described
as follows,
fk(x) = Re(Wk ∗ fk−1(x) + bk) (1)
where fk(x) represents the output of the kth feature extraction
layer, and f0(x) = x represents the input US image to the
network. Wk and bk denote the learnable weights and bias
in the kth convolution layer of the n etwork, respectively.
And ∗ denotes the convolution operation. Re denotes the
ReLU activation function. All feature extraction of the network
is performed in the LR space, wh ich can improve the training
efﬁciency of the network and the localization speed of each
frame.
2) Residual Learning : In theory, the deeper the network
is, the better the training performance can be. However,
in practice, when the gradient in each layer is < 1( > 1),
as the number of network layer increases, the gradient will
decay (increase) exponentially during training, which is called
gradient vanishing (exploding). In addition, with the network
depth increasing, the accuracy gets saturated and then degrades
rapidly because of a degradation p roblem. As a result, training
deeper networks becomes very difﬁcult [51].
To address the problem, in this work, the residual learning
(see the purple dotted box in Fig. 1 ) is introduced into the
proposed network. In detail, 5 residual blocks are added to
the network, and each contains 2 convolution layers, where
the input to the ﬁrst convolution layer is connected to the
output of the second convolution layer for the local residual
learning. The output of the ﬁrst convolution layer of the
entire network is connected to the output of the convolution
layer after the last residual block for the global residual
learning.
For each residual block, instead of directly mapping the
input to the output by stack layers, here, a residual mapping
is adopted which can be mathematically described as follows,
∂(κ)= ζ− κ (2)
where κ represents the input to the residual block, ζ represents
the output of the residual block, and ∂(κ) represents the
residual function. Once the residual mapping is built, we can
reconstruct the original mapping as ℑ(κ) = ζ = ∂(κ)+ κ.
Consequently, the task of learning a direct mapping (from the
input to the output) is converted to that of learning the residual
mapping (from the input to the residual).
According to [51], there are two beneﬁts associated with the
residual mapping. Firstly, it is easier to optimize the residual
mapping than to optimize the direct mapping (by using simple
stack layers). In other words, the residual network can help
overcome the gradient vanishing/exploding and degradation
problems, and makes it possible to train deeper network.
Secondly, the residual network can easily obtain accuracy
Authorized licensed use limited to: Soochow University. Downloaded on March 02,2026 at 05:57:20 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply. 
LIU et al.:D L F O R U L M 3067
gains with an increased depth, and produces better results
than the direct-mapping network. The detailed information
on residual learning block is described in Section I.D of the
Supplementary Material.
3) Sub-Pixel Convolution Layer : In US imaging, the experi-
mentally acquired US images are at low resolution. To achieve
a super-resolution US image (ULM), it is necessary to increase
the resolution of the LR image to match that of the SR image.
In this work, the upscaling operation is implemented by a
sub-pixel convolution layer.
Assume that the input image has a size of H × W,w h e r e
H and W are the height and width of the image, respectively.
When upscaling the input image by r times, we do not
directly generate the SR image . Comparably, the sub-pixel
convolution layer ﬁrst extracts H × W × r
2 feature maps from
the input through convolution. After that, the H × W × r2
elements are re-arranged and form a new image with a size
of ( r × H) × (r × W), which is implemented by periodic
shufﬂing (PS) operation. According to [50], the operation can
be mathematically described as follows,
ˆy = fl(x) = PS(Wl ∗ fl−1(x) + bl) (3)
where ˆy denotes the localization result of network prediction.
fl(x) and fl−1(x) represent the output of and the input to
the last convolution layer, respectively. Wl and bl denote the
corresponding learnable weights and bias. The schematic of
PS operation can be found in the red dotted box in Fig. 1 .
In this work, the upscale factor r is set as 4, which is selected
empirically through a comprehensive trial.
4) Loss Function : The choice of loss function has a great
impact on the network training. Although the mean squared
error (MSE) loss function has been widely used in DL,
the minimization of MSE may lead to smooth solutions that
ignore the high-frequency parts of the original signal [52].
Considering that in ULM imaging, MBs in the imaging
domain are generally very sparse, in this work, l

papers.id: MicroCT image-generated tumour geometry and SAR distribution for tumour temperature elevation simulations in magnetic nanoparticle hyperthermia.pdf-1758710138
papers.title: International Journal of Hyperthermia ISSN: 0265-6736 (Print) 1464-5157 (Online) Journal homepage: www.tandfonline.com/journals/ihyt20
papers.abstract_original:
Objectives: The objective of this study was to develop and test computer algorithms to export
micro computed tomography (microCT) images and to generate tumour geometry and specific
absorption rate (SAR) distribution for heat transfer simulation in magnetic nanoparticle
hyperthermia. Methods: Computer algorithms were written to analyse and export microCT
images of 3D tumours containing magnetic nanoparticles. MATLAB /C213and ProE/C213programs were
used to generate a prototype of the tumour geometry. The enhancements in the microCT pixel
index number due to presence of nanoparticles in the tumours were first converted into
corresponding SAR values. The SAR data were then averaged over three-dimensional clusters
of pixels using the SAS /C213program. This greatly decreased the size of the SAR file, while in the
meantime it ensured that the amount of total energy deposited in the tumour was conserved.
Both the tumour geometry and the SAR file were then imported into the COMSOL /C213software
package to simulate temperature elevations in the tumour and their surrounding tissue region
during magnetic nanoparticle hyperthermia. Results: A linear relationship was obtained to relate
individual pixel index numbers in the microCT images to the SAR values under a specific
magnetic field. The generated prototype of the tumour geometry based on only 30 slices
of microCT images resembled the original tumour shape and size. The tumour geometry
and the simplified SAR data set were successfully accepted by the COMSOL software for heat
transfer simulation. Up to 20 /C14C temperature elevations from its baseline temperature were
found inside the tumours, implying possible thermal damage to the tumour during magnetic
nanoparticle hyperthermia.
papers.abstract_summary_zh: 这篇文献主要围绕“其他”相关问题展开，核心对象是“International Journal of Hyperthermia ISSN: 0265-6736 (Print) 1464-5157 (Online) Journal homepage: www.tandfonline.com/journals/ihyt20”所对应的方法、材料或系统。作者重点讨论了该方向中的关键挑战、解决思路以及验证结果，适合用来快速把握这项工作的研究重点和应用价值。

papers.title: Photoacoustics 20 (2020) 100204 Available online 25 September 2020
papers.abstract_original:
Multispectral photoacoustic microscopy uses a wavelength-dependent absorption difference as a contrast 
mechanism to image the target molecule. In this paper, we present a novel multispectral pulsed fiber laser source, 
which selectively alternates the excitation wavelengths between green and red colors based on the stimulated 
Raman scattering (SRS) effect for imaging. This laser has a high pulse repetition rate (PRR) of 300 kHz and high 
pulse energy of more than 200 nJ meeting the real-time requirements of optical-resolution photoacoustic mi -
croscopy imaging. By switching the polarization state of the pump light and optical paths of the pump light, the 
operating wavelengths of the light source can be selectively alternated at the same fast PRR for any two SRS peak 
wavelengths between 545 and 655 nm. At 545 nm excitation wavelength, molecular photoacoustic signals from 
both blood vessels and gold nanorods were obtained simultaneously. However, at 655 nm, the photoacoustic 
signals of gold nanorods were dominant because the absorption of light by the blood vessels decreased drastically 
in the spectral region over 600 nm. Thus the multispectral photoacoustic system designed using the novel laser 
source implemented here could simultaneously monitor the time-dependent fast movement of two molecules 
independently, having different wavelength-dependent absorption properties at a high repetition rate of 0.49 
frames per second (fps).
papers.abstract_summary_zh: 这篇文献主要围绕“其他”相关问题展开，核心对象是“Photoacoustics 20 (2020) 100204 Available online 25 September 2020”所对应的方法、材料或系统。作者重点讨论了该方向中的关键挑战、解决思路以及验证结果，适合用来快速把握这项工作的研究重点和应用价值。

papers.title: Accurate and Real-Time Temperature Monitoring during MR Imaging Guided PTT
papers.abstract_original:
Photothermal therapy (PTT) is an e ﬃ cient
approach for cancer treatment. However, accurately monitoring
the spatial distribution of photothermal transducing agents (PTAs)
and mapping the real-time temperature change in tumor and
peritumoral normal tissue remain a huge challenge. Here, we
propose an innovative strategy to integrate T
1-MRI for precisely
tracking PTAs with magnetic resonance temperature imaging
(MRTI) for real-time monitoring temperature change in vivo
during PTT. NaBiF
4: Gd@PDA@PEG nanomaterials were
synthesized with favorable T 1-weighted performance to target
tumor and localize PTAs. The extremely weak susceptibility (1.04
× 10
−6 emu g−1 Oe1−) of NaBiF 4: Gd@PDA@PEG interferes with
the local phase marginally, which maintains the capability of MRTI
to dynamically record real-time temperature change in tumor and peritumoral normal tissue. The time resolution is 19 s per frame,
and the detection precision of temperature change is approximately 0.1 K. The approach achieving PTT guided by multimode MRI
holds signi ﬁcant potential for the clinical application.
papers.abstract_summary_zh: 这篇文献主要围绕“肿瘤消融”相关问题展开，核心对象是“Accurate and Real-Time Temperature Monitoring during MR Imaging Guided PTT”所对应的方法、材料或系统。作者重点讨论了该方向中的关键挑战、解决思路以及验证结果，适合用来快速把握这项工作的研究重点和应用价值。

papers.title: Contents lists available at ScienceDirect Photoacoustics
papers.abstract_original:
We report wide- ﬁeld polygon-scanning functional OR-PAM that for the ﬁrst time achieves 1-MHz A-line rate of
oxygen saturation in vivo. We address two technical challenges. The ﬁrst is a 1-MHz dual-wavelength pulsed
laser that has su ﬃcient pulse energy and ultrafast wavelength switching. The second is a polygon-scanning
imaging probe that has a fast scanning speed, a large ﬁeld of view, and great sensitivity. The OR-PAM system
oﬀers a B-scan rate of 477.5 Hz in a 12-mm range and a volumetric imaging rate of ∼1 Hz over a 12 × 5 mm 2
scanning area. We image microvasculature and blood oxygen saturation in a 12 × 12 mm 2 scanning area in 5 s.
Dynamic imaging of oxygen saturation in the mouse ear is demonstrated to monitor fast response to epinephrine
injection. The new wide-ﬁ eld fast functional imaging ability broadens the biomedical application of OR-PAM.

papers.title: 面向血液动力学的光声显微 成像技术 及其 应用研究
papers.abstract_original:
III 
 
Abstract 
Neuroimaging techniques are effective tools for investigating brain 
function. In the current neuroimaging field, mature technologies exist such as 
two-photon fluorescence imaging, wide -field fluorescence imaging, and 
electroencephalography, which can directly image and characterize micro -, 
meso -, and macro -level neural act ivity. However, for the other vital system in 
the brain, the vascular system, the current mature functional magnetic 
resonance imaging and functional ultrasound technologies only support research 
on main vessels or average hemodynamics. This creates ongoin g controversies 
regarding various blood transport issues related to brain function and makes it 
challenging to cross -validate neural activity and hemodynamic information at 
the microvascular level. Therefore, to further uncover the mystery of this 
complex system, there is an urgent need in neuroscience for a new imaging 
technique that can address hemodynamics and support micro and meso -level 
imaging. 
Photoacoustic microscopy is an effective solution to this problem. This 
imaging technique relies mainly on t he photoacoustic effect, imaging by 
receiving ultrasound signals emitted by chromophores excited by pulsed light. 
It has inherent characteristics suitable for biological imaging, such as label -free, 
radiation -free, and non -invasive features. The imaging co ntrast comes from the 
chromophore's absorption ability of laser, and the amplitude of the resulting 
photoacoustic signal directly indicates the concentration of hemoglobin , which 
is closely related to brain activity. In addition, the imaging resolution of this 
technology depends on the numerical aperture of the focusing objective and can 
reach submicron or even nanometer levels. The imaging speed can cover 
hemodynamic fluctuation frequencies, and the imaging field of view can also 
cover the mouse cortex. Ho wever, even though all the performance indicators of 
photoacoustic microscopy can meet the needs of brain function research, the 
imaging system is typically complex, voluminous, and inflexible, which limits 
its development in the field of neuroscience. 
http://kc.sustech.edu.cn

Abstract 
IV 
 
Therefore, this article conducted research on photoacoustic microscopy 
brain imaging technology, including feasibility demonstration, imaging system 
development, and analysis method establishment. Feasibility demonstration is 
the theoretical basis for all sub sequent research, which mainly deduces the 
generation principle of photoacoustic signals and the linear relationship 
between photoacoustic signals and chromophore concentration, demonstrates 
the feasibility of using photoacoustic signals to reflect hemoglo bin 
concentration, and the signal homogeneity of photoacoustic imaging technology 
and existing mature neuroimaging techniques. Based on theoretical research, 
this article developed a rotating optical resolution photoacoustic microscope 
suitable for imaging hemoglobin in the mouse cortex, with a two -dimensional 
scanning mirror and a line -focused ultrasound detector as the core devices and a 
novel rotating scanning mechanism as the imaging basis. Compared with 
classical photoacoustic microscopy imaging system s, the system integration and 
usability have been significantly improved, and high spatiotemporal resolution 
imaging of micro vessels within the mouse cortex can be easily achieved. In 
addition, this article added a dual -wavelength input module to the imag ing 
system to add blood oxygen saturation imaging function, and used 
photoacoustic correlation spectroscopy to add blood flow velocity imaging 
function. The cortical vascular photoacoustic images obtained by the imaging 
system are processed using both stru ctural and functional analysis methods, 
which can extract various structural indicators such as vascular area ratio, 
vascular length, vascular branching number, total number of vessels, and 
vascular diameter, as well as various brain activity functional in formation such 
as low -frequency amplitude, neurovascular coupling, and cortical functional 
connectivity, for a comprehensive analysis of the cortical vascular network 
from multiple perspectives. 
Based on the high -performance photoacoustic microscopy imagin g system 
and the accompanying data analysis methods, this article conducted in -depth 
research on multiple animal models, such as global epilepsy, focal epilepsy, 
local brain electrical stimulation, brain glioma, and local limb stimulation in 
mice. Specific ally, this article used functional analysis to demonstrate the vast 
difference in abnormal brain activity and functional connectivity between two 
http://kc.sustech.edu.cn

Abstract 
V 
 
epilepsy models and the propagation path of hemodynamic abnormalities in 
focal epilepsy. At the same time, by comparing the brain activity responses of 
focal epilepsy and local brain electrical stimulation, it revealed the long -range 
functional connections between the somatosensory cortex and motor cortex in 
the cerebral cortex. In addition, it quantitatively demo nstrated the pro -growth 
effect of brain gliomas on the cortical vascular network, the interference effect 
on cortical hemodynamic responses, and the destructive effect on regional and 
hemispheric functional connections. 
In summary, this article designed an d developed a photoacoustic 
microscopy imaging system suitable for mouse brain function research, as well 
as accompanying data analysis methods, and conducted brain function research 
on various mouse models. The theoretical analysis and application researc h 
conducted in this article fully demonstrate the brain function research 
capabilities of the photoacoustic microscopy brain imaging technology 
developed in this article, laying a solid foundation for the progress of imaging 
systems in photoacoustic imagin g and providing a new method for mesoscopic 
brain functional imaging based on hemodynamics for neuroscience research.

papers.title: Dexterous hand towards intelligent manufacturing: A review of technologies, trends, and potential applications
papers.abstract_original: Multi-fingered dexterous hands hold significant potential for addressing manipulation tasks in intelligent manufacturing, owing to their inherent anthropomorphic flexibility and rich perceptual capabilities. However, the functionality of dexterous hands in intelligent manufacturing remains unclear due to current technological limitations. This paper provides a review of the current research status of dexterous hands for manipulation tasks, with a specific focus on key technologies related to structure, perception, grasping, and manipulation skills. Furthermore, this review also highlights potential applications of dexterous hands in intelligent manufacturing, including manufacturing in narrow spaces, bin-picking, and in-situ manufacturing. We also discuss the research challenges for the wide adoption of dexterous hands. This review offers a comprehensive analysis of dexterous hands from a manufacturing perspective, contributing to future technological breakthroughs and the advancement of new-generation intelligent manufacturing.
papers.abstract_summary_zh: 这篇文献是一篇面向智能制造场景的灵巧手综述，重点梳理了多指灵巧手在结构设计、感知能力、抓取与操作技能方面的发展现状。它关注的核心问题是，灵巧手虽然具有人形手的灵活性和感知潜力，但距离在真实制造环境中大规模落地仍有明显技术差距。文章同时讨论了狭小空间作业、bin-picking 和原位制造等潜在应用，对判断灵巧手在智能制造中的发展方向和关键瓶颈很有参考价值。
papers.source_note: 自动解析导入，后经人工校正作者、标签与摘要

papers.title: Multi-fingered Hand Grasps with Visuo-Tactile Fusion via Multi-Agent Deep Reinforcement Learning
papers.abstract_original: Humans achieve contact-rich dexterous grasping through the synergy of visual and tactile information. However, the high-dimensional action space of high DoF multi-fingered hands poses significant challenges to this operation. In this study, we address this complexity by controlling the robotic hand at the reduced dimensional level of individual fingers instead of the entire hand, and develop a finger-based multi-agent deep reinforcement learning strategy by regarding the wrist, arm, and each finger of the hand as intelligent agents. We commence by applying a single-agent reinforcement learning algorithm to guide the whole hand to reach the feasible approaching direction and distance to the object. Then, we develop neuroscience-inspired visuo-tactile fusion networks to train multiple agents to control their assigned fingers by effectively leveraging visual and tactile feedback. This enables dynamic and collaborative adjustments of finger-object interactions, ultimately achieving precise contact with specific areas of the objects. The grasping results on 8 objects show that our approach can achieve stable and compliant grasps. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work that employs a finger-based multi-agent reinforcement learning approach to control the dexterous grasping process under the guidance of both visual and tactile feedback.
papers.abstract_summary_zh: 这篇文献研究的是多指灵巧手在视觉和触觉共同引导下的稳定抓取问题，核心方法是把手腕、机械臂和每根手指都视为独立智能体，用多智能体深度强化学习来协同控制。作者先让整只手完成接近物体的动作，再通过视觉—触觉融合网络细化各手指的接触与调整，从而降低高自由度抓取的控制难度。它的价值在于为灵巧手抓取提供了一种更接近人类感知协同方式的控制框架，对机器人精细操作和触觉感知抓取都有参考意义。

papers.title: Injectable bioresorbable conductive hydrogels for multimodal brain tumor electroimmunotherapy
papers.abstract_original: Current electrode technologies are too rigid for safe and effective delivery of electrotherapy in the brain, and patients with glioblastoma continue to face a devastating prognosis, with median survival stalled at 15 months despite intensive treatment with surgery, radiation, and chemotherapy. But these conventional approaches potentially compromise immune function, underscoring the urgent need for therapies that activate, rather than suppress, the immune system. Therefore, we introduce injectable conductive hydrogels engineered to match the softness of brain tissue while exhibiting electrical conductivities up to three orders of magnitude higher than any previously reported injectable hydrogels. They can be implanted through minimally invasive syringe capillaries as narrow as 30 µm, avoiding brain damage while enabling local and conformal delivery. The work positions these bioresorbable conductive hydrogels as a platform for multimodal brain tumor electroimmunotherapy.
papers.abstract_summary_zh: 这篇文献提出了一种可注射、可吸收、具高导电性的脑组织适配水凝胶，用于脑肿瘤的多模态电免疫治疗。它关注的核心问题，是传统脑部电极过于刚硬、难以安全贴合组织，同时现有治疗手段对免疫系统支持不足。作者通过构建柔软且高导电的水凝胶材料，尝试实现更微创、更局部、更贴合脑组织的治疗递送方式，对脑肿瘤治疗器械与材料一体化设计有明显参考价值。

papers.title: Time-Resolved Ratiometric Fluorescence Nanothermometer for Real-Time Endoscopic Temperature Guidance during Tumor Ablation
papers.abstract_original: Thermal ablation is a common treatment option for early-stage cancers, but the lack of real-time temperature imaging feedback method increases the risk of incomplete or excessive ablation. Although ratiometric nanothermometer offers a rapid temperature imaging solution, accurate in vivo signal extraction remains challenging due to the autofluorescence and wavelength-dependent tissue absorption and scattering. Herein, a time-resolved ratiometric fluorescence nanothermometer composed of europium and iridium complex with identical working wavelength but distinguishing lifetimes is reported, whose well-designed structures enable 450 nm excitation of both complexes with a high quantum yield (57.8%). Based on the nanothermometer, accurate signal extraction is realized in whole blood, beneath a 2 cm tissue phantom and a 5 mm pork slice through a time-resolved ratiometric method. By leveraging the exceptional thermal sensitivity (6.9% K−1), high temperature resolution (0.02 K), and clinically relevant temperature range (30–96 °C) of the nanothermometer, a fluorescence temperature endoscopy system is further designed with a real-time temperature imaging speed of 10 fps, which is applied to minimally invasive temperature monitoring during microwave ablation of liver tumors in rabbits, realizing precise ablation control through dynamic ablation power adjustment. The real-time and accurate temperature imaging performance of the nanothermometer may offer a new perspective for intraoperative guidance.
papers.abstract_summary_zh: 这篇文献提出了一种用于肿瘤消融术中温度引导的时间分辨比率型荧光纳米温度计，并进一步做成了可实时成像的内窥温度监测系统。它要解决的核心问题，是在体内组织自发荧光、吸收和散射干扰下，如何稳定、准确地获得消融区域温度。作者通过寿命可区分但工作波长一致的铕/铱复合体系，实现了更可靠的温度读出，并在动物肝肿瘤微波消融中验证了对消融过程精细控制的价值。
